coredata.dc:description: In the current work, sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (S,N-CDs) as simple, sensitive, and selective turn-off fluorescent nanosensors were utilized for analysis of three phenothiazine derivatives, including acetophenazine (APZ), chlorpromazine (CPH), and promethazine (PZH). S,N-CDs were synthesized through a green one-pot microwave-assisted technique using widely available precursors (thiourea and ascorbic acid). HRTEM, EDX, FTIR spectroscopy, UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy were used to characterize the as-synthesized CDs. When excited at 330 nm, the carbon dots produced a maximum emission peak at 410 nm. The cited drugs statically quenched the S,N-CDs fluorescence as revealed by the Stern-Volmer equation. The current method represents the first spectrofluorimetric approach for the determination of the studied drugs without the need for chemical derivatization or harsh reaction conditions. The importance of the proposed work is magnified as the cited drugs do not have any fluorescent properties. The fluorescence of the developed sensor exhibited a linear response to APZ, CPH, and PZH in the concentration ranges of 5.0–100.0, 10.0–100.0, and 10.0–200.0 μM with detection limits of 1.53, 1.66, and 2.47 μM, respectively. The developed fluorescent probes have the advantages of rapidity and selectivity for APZ, CPH, and PZH analysis in tablets with acceptable % recoveries of (98.06–101.66 %). Evaluation of the method's greenness was performed using the Complementary Green Analytical Procedure Index (ComplexGAPI) and Analytical GREEnness metric (AGREE) metrics, indicating that the method is environmentally friendly. Validation of the proposed method was performed according to ICHQ2 (R1) guidelines.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the current work, sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (S,N-CDs) as simple, sensitive, and selective turn-off fluorescent nanosensors were utilized for analysis of three phenothiazine derivatives, including acetophenazine (APZ), chlorpromazine (CPH), and promethazine (PZH). S,N-CDs were synthesized through a green one-pot microwave-assisted technique using widely available precursors (thiourea and ascorbic acid). HRTEM, EDX, FTIR spectroscopy, UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy were used to characterize the as-synthesized CDs. When excited at 330 nm, the carbon dots produced a maximum emission peak at 410 nm. The cited drugs statically quenched the S,N-CDs fluorescence as revealed by the Stern-Volmer equation. The current method represents the first spectrofluorimetric approach for the determination of the studied drugs without the need for chemical derivatization or harsh reaction conditions. The importance of the proposed work is magnified as the cited drugs do not have any fluorescent properties. The fluorescence of the developed sensor exhibited a linear response to APZ, CPH, and PZH in the concentration ranges of 5.0–100.0, 10.0–100.0, and 10.0–200.0 μM with detection limits of 1.53, 1.66, and 2.47 μM, respectively. The developed fluorescent probes have the advantages of rapidity and selectivity for APZ, CPH, and PZH analysis in tablets with acceptable % recoveries of (98.06–101.66 %). Evaluation of the method's greenness was performed using the Complementary Green Analytical Procedure Index (ComplexGAPI) and Analytical GREEnness metric (AGREE) metrics, indicating that the method is environmentally friendly. Validation of the proposed method was performed according to ICHQ2 (R1) guidelines.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Phenothiazine derivatives are organic compounds of potent physiological activity, used as antipsychotics, antihistaminics, tranquilizers, and antiemetics [1]. Acetophenazine (APZ), chlorpromazine hydrochloride (CPH), and promethazine hydrochloride (PZH) are three important phenothiazines that are used in the fields of psychiatry and allergy treatment, respectively.

Acetophenazine (APZ) is an antipsychotic drug used to treat psychosis, schizophrenia, thinking disorders, and false perceptions, such as delusions or hallucinations [2]. It also has antihistaminic, alpha-adrenergic, and anticholinergic effects [3]. Chemically, APZ is 1-[10-[3-[4-(2-hydroxyethyl)piperazin-1-yl]propyl]phenothiazin-2-yl]ethanone [4] (Fig. 1
a). According to a review of literature, LC/MS [5,6] are among the few analytical methods reported for its determination. Consequently, developing a novel simpler method for APZ analysis without the need for complicated techniques is required.

Chlorpromazine hydrochloride (CPH) is an antipsychotic medication used for the treatment of psychotic disorders, schizophrenia, severe behavioral disorders in children, and manic phase of bipolar disorders [7]. CPH acts as dopamine-2 receptors antagonist and also has antihistaminic and anti-serotonergic properties. Chemically, CPH is 3-(2-chlorophenothiazin-10-yl)-N,N-dimethylpropan-1-amine;hydrochloride [8] ( Fig. 1 b). Several methods were reported for its estimation, viz, LC/MS [9,10], HPLC [11], GC [12], electrochemical [13], spectrophotometric [14], and spectrofluorimetric methods [15].

Promethazine hydrochloride (PZH) is a first-generation antihistaminic drug. Although PZH was first developed as an antipsychotic drug, it is still used to treat a variety of mental diseases, such as schizophrenia [1,16]. It has also antiemetic, analgesic, sedative, antipruritic, and anti-motion sickness effects. Chemically, PZH is N,N-dimethyl-1-phenothiazin-10-ylpropan-2-amine;hydrochloride [17] (Fig. 1 c). Various methods for PZH assessment were reported, LC/MS [18,19], HPLC [20,21], GC [22], electrochemical methods [23,24], spectrophotometric [25–27], and spectrofluorimetric methods [15,28,29] are among these approaches. Compared to the reported methods [18–24,5,6,9–13] that need expensive equipment and tedious experimental steps, the current method proposes a simple spectrofluorimetric method as an efficient analytical solution for the analysis of the cited drugs.

The spectrofluorimetric technique offers rapid measurements, great sensitivity, and exceptional selectivity among its many advantages. It does not require the preparation and conditioning of a mobile phase as in the chromatographic techniques. Furthermore, its applicability and eco-friendliness are enhanced, as it doesn't need excessive amounts of organic solvents or high-cost instrumentation. Therefore, the spectrofluorimetric approach was widely applied to analyze various medicinal substances in diverse matrices [30–46].

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) are promising nanomaterials having many properties, such as chemical inertness, good biocompatibility, excellent solubility in water, high photo-bleaching resistance, negligible toxicity, and high-stability structure [47,48]. CDs had attracted a great interest in various applications, such as photo-catalysis, photo-voltaics, drug delivery, bio-imaging, and sensors. Doping with heteroatoms, as phosphorus (P), sulfur (S), and nitrogen (N) improves the properties of CDs to a great extent [49–52]. Sulfur or Nitrogen co-doped CDs are the most widely investigated, since S and C are close in electronegativity, meanwhile, N and C have actually the same radius [50,53]. Many synthetic routes have been conducted to produce CDs, such as electrochemical carbonization, laser-ablation, hydro/solvothermal method, and chemical oxidation [54]. Most of these techniques require the use of hazardous and expensive materials, complex procedures, energy-consuming devices, and drastic synthetic conditions. On the other hand, the microwave-assisted technique has several advantages, like reduced energy consumption, improved uniformity of products size, enhanced reaction rates, greater ease of manipulation, and increased product yields [33,54–56].

In this work, a one-step facile microwave-assisted process was utilized to prepare sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (S,N-CDs) using thiourea (as sulfur and nitrogen source) and ascorbic acid (as carbon source). This technique could be accomplished within 5 min using a domestic microwave, which opens up a safe and rapid way for preparing heteroatom doped carbon dots. The synthesized S,N-CDs were effectively implemented as fluorescent probes for determination of the studied compounds as the fluorescence of S,N-CDs was quantitatively quenched with increasing their concentrations. It is worth noting that, no spectrofluorimetric methods have been reported for the analysis of APZ and the reported spectrofluorimetric methods for determination of CPH and PZH required pre-derivatization or tedious procedures [15,28,29]. Consequently, the objective of the current work was to develop a novel fluorescence quenching-based method for determination of the cited drugs in their raw materials and pharmaceutical dosage forms with high selectivity and recovery. In addition, the method's greenness and ecofriendliness were assessed using Complementary Green Analytical Procedure Index (ComplexGAPI) [57] and Analytical GREEnness metric (AGREE) [58] metrics, indicating the method's excellent greenness profile. Therefore, the proposed method can be efficiently applied for the routine analysis of the studied drugs in quality control laboratories.

Double-beam spectrophotometer (PG Instrument, Wibtoft, UK) was utilized for measurement of UV–Vis absorption spectra of the analytes. All fluorescence intensities and spectra were attained by Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer from Agilent Technologies (Santa Clara, CA 95051, USA), equipped with Xenon light sourceat voltage of 730 V, and a slit width set at 5 nm. The surface morphology of S,N-CDs was examined with High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) by JEOL, JEM-2100 (Tokyo, Japan), operated at 200 KV. Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy attached to JSM-IT100LA Scanning Electron Microscope (JEOL, Japan) was used to determine the elemental composition of S,N-CDs.

The surface functional groups of S,N-CDs were examined using Nicolet-IS10 Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) Spectrometer from Thermo Fisher Scientific (USA). The measurements were recorded as 32 scans at resolution of 8 cm−1 across a spectral range of 4000 to 400 cm−1. A domestic microwave oven (900 W) was used for preparation of S,N-CDs. Ultrasonic bath sonicator (model SS-101H-230, USA), Cooling centrifuge (model 2-16P, Germany), 0.45 µm syringe filters (Phenomenex, USA), and Jenway pH-Meter (model 3510, UK) were also utilized.

Acetophenazine (99.25 %), chlorpromazine hydrochloride (99.20 %) and promethazine hydrochloride (99.0 %) were kindly provided by Misr Pharmaceutical Company (Cairo, Egypt). Neurazine® tablets (100 mg CPH/tablet, Batch No.126100) were obtained from a local Pharmacy. Thiourea, ascorbic acid, methanol, acetic acid, boric acid, sodium hydroxide, quinine sulphate, phosphoric acid, and potassium dihydrogen phosphate were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Barium chloride, calcium chloride, sodium carbonate, magnesium sulphate, citric acid, maltose, dextrin, mannitol, talc, maize starch, lactose, magnesium stearate, and sulfuric acid were purchased from Al-Gomhoria Chemical Company (Cairo, Egypt). All chemicals and materials used were of analytical grade and double-distilled water was utilized throughout the study.

Britton-Robinson buffer (0.02 M) was prepared in double-distilled water using a mixture of 0.04 M boric acid, 0.04 M acetic acid, and 0.04 M phosphoric acid, and the pH was adjusted using 0.2 M NaOH solution to cover a pH range of 2–12.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for the synthesis of S,N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
S,N-CDs synthesis was performed using two main precursors, thiourea and ascorbic acid. Typically, 0.5 g of each of thiourea and ascorbic acid were heated in a domestic microwave for 5 min until completely charred. The mixture was cooled at room temperature, then 30 mL aliquot of double-distilled water was added, followed by sonication for 30 min. The synthesized S,N-CDs were centrifuged for 10 min at 6000 rpm to remove any foreign particles then the clear supernatant was filtered using 0.45 μm membrane filter. The prepared S,N-CDs solution was kept at 4 °C for future use (Fig. 2
).

Stock solutions (1.0 mM) were separately prepared by dissolving accurately weighed amounts of APZ, CPH, and PZH in methanol. Subsequent dilution of the stock solutions was made with double-distilled water for preparation of working solutions of concentrations over the range of 5.0–100.0, 10.0–100.0, and 10.0–200.0 μM for APZ, CPH, and PZH, respectively. The obtained solutions were stable for at least 10 days under refrigeration.

The quantum yield of the prepared carbon quantum dots (ΦS,N-CDs) was evaluated using quinine sulphate (ΦQS = 54 %) as a standard fluorophore by applying the single point method. The fluorescence spectra of S,N-CDs solution and quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4, and the absorbance values were measured. The following formula was used to determine ΦS,N-CDs [59,60]:

Freeze-drying of S,N-CDs solution was performed to produce S,N-CDs solid, and the production yield (PY) was calculated after weighing the prepared solid. The following equation was used to determine PY [61]:

where, mS,N-CDs represents the S,N-CDs solid mass, and ms represents the precursors mass (thiourea and ascorbic acid).

Into 10-mL volumetric flasks, accurately measured volumes of standard solutions were added, then 100.0 μL aliquots of S,N-CDs solution were added, then completed to the volume with double-distilled water to attain the required concentrations over the ranges of 5.0–100.0 μM for APZ, 10.0–100.0 μM for CPH, and 10.0–200.0 μM for PZH. The fluorescence intensities measurement was performed at 410 nm after excitation at 330 nm. The calibration curves between the fluorescence quenching values (ΔF = F0-F) and drugs concentration (μM) were plotted, and their regression equations were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the drugs in their tablets
ce:section.ce:para: As the commercial tablet dosage forms for APZ and PZH were not available in the Egyptian market, laboratory-prepared APZ and PZH tablets were obtained by mixing with maize starch (15 mg), talc (20 mg), lactose (15 mg), and magnesium stearate (10 mg) for each prepared tablet. An accurately weighed quantity of the prepared tablets equivalent to each of 20.0 mg APZ and 25.0 mg PZH was transferred into a small conical flask, then 50 mL aliquot of methanol was added, followed by ultra-sonication for 10 min. Filtration of the resulting solution was carried out into 100-mL volumetric flask, then completed with methanol to the mark. Aliquot volumes of the filtrate were analyzed according to the procedures under Section “2.7.1” . The regression equations were used to determine the tablets' nominal contents.

Ten of Neurazine® tablets were accurately weighed and finely ground. An accurately weighed quantity of powder corresponding to 100 mg of CPH was transferred into a small conical flask, then continued as mentioned above.
S,N-CDs were thoroughly characterized with HRTEM, EDX, FTIR, UV–Visible absorption spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy as follows:

The surface morphology of the as-prepared S,N-CDs was examined using HRTEM. The examination of the sample was carried out at 200 KV operating voltage. HRTEM images are presented in Fig. 3
a, which revealed that, S,N-CDs were uniform with quasi-spherical shapes with a size in a range of 8–12 nm. EDX spectroscopy was utilized to determine the elemental composition of the synthesized S,N-CDs, indicating that they were mostly composed of carbon, sulfur, nitrogen, and oxygen, as presented in Fig. 3 b.

The surface moieties of S,N-CDs were analyzed using FTIR. Fig. 3 c displays their FTIR diagram where the peaks at 3406 cm−1 and 3192 cm−1 are due to N–H/O–H groups, implying that the prepared S,N-CDs have plenty of amino and hydroxyl groups, which are responsible for the excellent hydrophilicity of the dots [62]. The peak at 2055 cm−1 is due to C-N group, while the vibrational peak centered at 1659 cm−1 is due to C = O group. Also, the peaks centered at 1501 cm−1, 1186 cm−1, and 639 cm−1 are attributed to the presence of C = C, C = S, and C–H groups, respectively [63].

In addition, the optical characteristics of S,N-CDs were further characterized by UV–Vis spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of S,N-CDs is presented in Fig. 4
a, showing a characteristic peak at 242 nm. The solution of the as-synthesized S,N-CDs exhibited blue fluorescence under UV light and dark orange color under visible light (Inset of Fig. 4 a), and the solution was very stable without any precipitation for at least 2 months. The prepared S,N-CDs showed a maximum native fluorescence at 410 nm when excited at 330 nm, as shown in Fig. 4 b. The fluorescence spectra were investigated at different excitation wavelengths through the 300 to 360 nm range, and the maximum fluorescence emission was recorded at λexcitation of 330 nm, as shown in Fig. 4 c. The QY of S,N-CDs was calculated and found to be as high as 28 % when compared with quinine sulfate as a reference, indicating that the prepared S,N-CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence properties. The PY of S,N-CDs was also determined and found to be 18 ± 3 wt%.

The three drugs have efficient quenching impact on the native fluorescence of S,N-CDs, enabling the usage of these CDs as fluorescent probes for their analysis. Fig. 1 illustrates that the intrinsic fluorescence of the synthesized S,N-CDs was quantitatively decreased with increasing concentrations of the cited drugs. Mechanisms of quenching of CDs may be inner filter effect (IFE), dynamic quenching, and static quenching [64,65]. The equation of Stern-Volmer was applied to determine the potential fluorescence quenching mechanism [66]:
(3)

where, F and F0 denote the fluorescence intensities of S,N-CDs at λexcitation/λemission of 330 nm/410 nm after and before addition of the cited drugs, respectively; Ksv represents the Stern-Volmer constant; [Q] denotes the APZ or CPH or PZH molar concentration; kq refers to the fluorescence quenching constant; τ0 refers to the average lifetime (10-8 s) [67].

The values of Ksv were calculated for AZP, CPH, and PZH at three different temperature settings (303, 313, 323 K) (Table 1 ). As presented in Table 1 and Fig. 5 , Ksv values decrease with increasing temperature, indicating that the mechanism of quenching with the three drugs was static quenching [64]. The UV spectra of each of APZ, CPH, and PZH almost overlap with that of S,N-CDs excitation spectrum (Fig. 6
), thus pointing out to the possibility of IFE mechanism. The following equation was applied to correct the S,N-CDs fluorescence intensity upon addition of various concentrations of each drug [68]:

where, Fobs and Fcorr represent the observed and corrected fluorescence intensity, respectively. Aem is the value of absorbance of each drug at 410 nm, and Aex is the value of its absorbance at 330 nm.

The suppressed efficiency (%E) was determined for both observed and corrected fluorescence intensities, and then %E values were plotted versus drugs concentrations. The following equation was used to calculate %E [69–71]:

It is worth noting that, IFE mechanism has an impact on the quenching process, as presented in Fig. 7 . Therefore, it could be concluded that the quenching mechanism is static quenching affected by IFE.

To investigate the pH effect on the quenching of S,N-CDs fluorescence by the cited drugs, Britton-Robison buffer over the range of 2–12 was used. There was no significant effect on the obtained results upon changing pH ( Fig. 8
a), therefore, no buffer was needed throughout the study; which made the developed method more facile.

The effect of incubation period was noticed after addition of the studied drugs to S,N-CDs at time intervals between 1 min and 30 min. As illustrated in Fig. 8 b, the reaction between S,N-CDs and the drugs was fast and accomplished in 1 min, and the response of the probe remained stable for at least 30 min.

The influence of temperature was also studied over the range of 25–50 °C. It was found that, the quenching of fluorescence was gradually reduced upon increasing temperature for the three drugs ( Fig. 8 c), so the study was performed at room temperature.
The validation of the proposed method was tested according to the validation guidelines standardized by the International Council of Harmonization (ICH) [72].

The linearity and range were determined by plotting F0-F against the drugs concentration (µM). The regression plot showed a linear relationship within the ranges of 5.0–100.0, 10.0–100.0, and 10.0–200.0 μM for APZ, CPH, and PZH, respectively, as illustrated in Table 2 . The following equations resulted from a linear regression evaluation of the data:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantitation (LOQ)
ce:section.ce:para: LOD and LOQ were calculated from the equations: 3.3 σ/slope and 10 σ/slope, respectively, whereas σ represents the standard deviation (SD) of the intercept of the regression line. As abridged in Table 2, the measured LOD and LOQ values showed acceptable sensitivity of the proposed method for analysis of the studied drugs.

Robustness of the proposed method was examined by studying the effect of minor changes in the reagent volume (100 μL ± 5 μL) and the incubation period (1 min ± 0.5 min) on the fluorescence quenching by the drugs. It is worth noting that, the analytical response of the drugs did not change significantly with the slight variations in the experimental conditions, indicating the method's robustness (Table 5 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the drugs in tablets
ce:section.ce:para: The current work was applied for the analysis of the studied drugs in their tablets with acceptable % recoveries of 100.15 ± 1.58, 99.72 ± 1.34, and 100.23 ± 1.63 for APZ, CPH, and PZH, respectively and low % RSD < 2 % (Table 7 ). The results were statistically evaluated with the Student's t-test and the Variance ratio F-test and the developed and comparison methods' results [73,74] showed that no marked difference was obtained in terms of accuracy and precision, respectively.

With the aim of evaluating the greenness of the developed method, ComplexGAPI [57] and AGREE [58] were applied. AGREE is a flexible, comprehensive, and straightforward evaluation tool that provides informative and obvious results, and can be applied to assess the influence of each of the twelve green analytical chemistry (GAC) principles on a unified 0–1 scale. The mean advantages of AGREE are easy to perform (with a simple software) and easy to interpret (the colored pictogram that illustrates the structures of strong and weak points). ComplexGAPI is a simple modification of the original GAPI [77] through adding a hexagonal field to GAPI pictogram. ComplexGAPI reflects the greenness aspect of all phases of the analytical process as well as the pre-analysis processes (conditions, reagents, instruments, work up, and purification). It also evaluates the synthesis of stationary phases, organic compounds, and nanomaterials. The adopted method used one-pot microwave-assisted carbonization of ascorbic acid and thiourea in water as a green solvent for the synthesis of S,N-CDs, that was used for direct detection of the studied drugs. As illustrated in Fig. 10 , the method is green with a green colored complexGAPI pictogram and a high AGREE score of 0.8.

The developed method provides a simple, rapid, and eco-friendly spectrofluorimetric method for the analysis of the cited drugs with acceptable sensitivity, unlike the reported chromatographic and electrochemical methods, which need expensive equipment and tedious experimental steps. A comparison of the proposed method with the reported ones for the determination of CPH, PZH, and APZ is presented in Table 8 . The proposed method could determine CPH and PZH with high sensitivity and LOD of 1.66 µM (0.58 μg/mL) and 2.47 µM (0.79 μg/mL), respectively, which is 1.2–5 times more sensitive than the previously reported methods [11,25,27], as presented in Table 8. For the previously reported method [15] for estimation of PZH, however, it is more sensitive than the proposed method; it is time-consuming and requires pre-derivatization steps. For APZ, only two LC-MS methods were reported for its analysis [5,6], but the developed method is the first spectrofluorimetric approach for its estimation without the need for large volumes of organic solvents or high-cost instrumentation. Therefore, the proposed method is superior to the previously reported methods for the analysis of the cited drugs.

In this work, rapid and simple microwave-assisted synthesis of S,N-CDs yielded fluorescent nanosensor that was used for each of APZ, CPH, and PZH sensing. The combination of thiourea and ascorbic acid is economic, supplying nitrogen, sulfur, and carbon to synthesize S,N-CDs with excellent fluorescence. Different microscopic and spectroscopic techniques were utilized to examine the optical characteristics of the synthesized S,N-CDs. On increasing the concentrations of the studied drugs, the fluorescence of the S,N-CDs was obviously reduced as a result of static quenching mechanism and a complementary inner filter effect. The developed method was efficiently used to assess APZ, CPH, and PZH in pure forms and tablets without interference from common additives with minimal % RSD values and high % recoveries (98.06–101.66 %). The developed probe has the advantages of rapidity and simplicity for estimation of the studied drugs without the need for harsh reaction conditions or chemical derivatization that are needed in the previously reported techniques. ComplexGAPI and AGREE metrics were also applied to evaluate the greenness of the developed method.

Fathalla Belal: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Conceptualization.
